content:
First observe that

\[
\frac{(ab+1)(bc+1)(ca+1)}{abc}
\,=\,abc+\bigl(a+b+c\bigr)+\bigl(\tfrac1a+\tfrac1b+\tfrac1c\bigr)+\tfrac1{abc},
\]

so in order for it to be an integer we need

\[
g=\frac1a+\frac1b+\frac1c+\frac1{abc}\in\mathbb{Z}.
\]

Since \(a,b,c\) are positive, one checks:

1. If \(a,b,c\ge2\) then 
   \[
   g\le\frac12+\frac12+\frac12+\frac1{8}=1.625<2,
   \]
   so the only possible integer value is \(g=1\).  That leads to
   \[
   bc+ca+ab+1=abc
   \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
   abc-(ab+bc+ca)=1,
   \]
   whose only solution in integers \(\ge2\) is \(\{a,b,c\}=\{2,3,7\}\).  This gives \(3!=6\) ordered triples.

2. If at least one of \(a,b,c\) equals 1, one checks by cases:

   (i)  All three are 1:  \((1,1,1)\) works.  
   (ii) Exactly two are 1:  say \(a=b=1\), \(c>1\).  Then 
        \(g=2+2/c\) is integral only for \(c=2\).  By symmetry this gives  
        \((1,1,2)\), \((1,2,1)\), \((2,1,1)\), i.e.\ 3 more.  
   (iii) Exactly one is 1:  say \(a=1\), \(b,c\ge2\).  Then one needs
        \(\tfrac1b+\tfrac1c+\tfrac1{bc}=1\), i.e.\ \((b-1)(c-1)=2\),
        so \((b,c)=(2,3)\) or \((3,2)\).  That gives \(2\) for each choice
        of which variable is 1, hence \(3\cdot2=6\) more.

Altogether
\[
6\;(\text{from }2,3,7)
\;+\;1\;+\;3\;+\;6
\;=\;16
\]
ordered triples.  Therefore the answer is

\[
\boxed{16}.
\]
